passages.text: A contrastive learning framework with adaptive feature fusion for brain tumor classification

Accurate classification of brain tumors from MRI scans is critical for diagnosis and treatment planning, yet remains challenging due to high intra-class heterogeneity and subtle inter-class differences. While deep learning has shown promise in automating this task, existing models often lack the discriminative power to reliably capture fine-grained pathological features. To address these limitations, we propose a novel contrastive learning framework with adaptive feature fusion (AFF-CL). Our method introduces a dynamic label queue that stores historical labels, enabling the construction of multiple positive pairs from different images of the same class. This design injects explicit supervision into contrastive learning, where the multiple positive pairs provide comprehensive intra-class variation coverage, directly facilitating feature representations that minimize intra-class distance while maximizing inter-class separation. Furthermore, to capture multi-scale contextual information, we augment the model with a local input stream and design an adaptive feature fusion (AFF) module to intelligently integrate localized features with global representations. Extensive experiments on the public figshare dataset demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods. The proposed approach shows strong potential for enhancing the precision of computer-aided diagnosis in clinical practice.
Introduction

Brain tumors represent one of the most formidable challenges in modern oncology, characterized by the uncontrolled proliferation of cells within the cranial cavity. The growth of these abnormal masses can impose mechanical pressure on vital neural structures. This leads to a spectrum of neurological impairments, including severe headaches, seizures, and sensory-motor deficits. Clinically, tumors range from benign (slow-growing) to malignant (aggressive). To standardize diagnosis and prognosis, the World Health Organization (WHO) has established a comprehensive grading system. In clinical practice, Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) serves as the cornerstone for brain tumor detection and evaluation, providing exceptional detail of soft tissue anatomy. However, the diagnostic process remains heavily reliant on manual interpretation by radiologists. This practice is inherently subjective, time-consuming, and prone to inter-observer variability. These challenges are particularly acute when distinguishing between common tumor types, such as meningioma, glioma, and pituitary tumors. The difficulty arises from their often overlapping radiographic appearances and significant intra-class heterogeneity.

The advent of deep learning, encompassing convolutional neural networks (CNNs), has heralded a new era in medical image analysis, promising to automate and augment diagnostic workflows. Moreover, significant advancements have been achieved in discriminative feature learning, particularly for fine-grained image recognition, retrieval, and robust generalization, providing valuable methodological inspirations for capturing subtle pathological variations. These models excel at learning hierarchical feature representations from vast amounts of imaging data. Nevertheless, prevailing deep learning models frequently struggle with the specific complexities of brain tumor MRI analysis. While strategies like the integration of attention modules and graph structures have been employed to boost performance, existing models still exhibit non-trivial error rates. These errors frequently stem from the model’s failure to capture the nuanced pathological distinctions between tumor categories. This limitation is exacerbated by two inherent characteristics of brain MRI data. First, standard clinical imaging protocols acquire scans from multiple anatomical planes, including axial, coronal, and sagittal views. This creates substantial intra-class heterogeneity, as the same tumor type exhibits diverse textural and morphological presentations across different imaging perspectives. Second, discriminative features between tumor classes often reside in subtle variations in spatial distribution, morphological structure, and boundary characteristics rather than conspicuous visual differences. This presents challenging fine-grained inter-class variations that conventional models struggle to resolve.

To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel contrastive learning framework with adaptive feature fusion, named AFF-CL, for brain tumor classification. Our method leverages a dynamic label queue and a corresponding feature queue. Unlike conventional contrastive learning that primarily relies on self-augmentations to form positive pairs, our framework capitalizes on the stored label information in the queue. This allows us to dynamically construct multiple positive sample pairs from different images belonging to the same class. This mechanism injects explicit supervisory signals directly into the contrastive learning process. Effectively, it guides the model to learn more discriminative and semantically meaningful representations by pulling features of the same tumor type closer in the embedding space while pushing others apart. Furthermore, to capture richer, multi-scale representations that are crucial for discerning subtle pathological details, we augment the standard global encoding pipeline with an additional local input stream. These local inputs are derived from focused regions of the original MRI scans, forcing the model to scrutinize fine-grained patterns. A critical component of our architecture is the designed adaptive feature fusion (AFF) module. This module is responsible for intelligently and dynamically integrating the global contextual information with these localized details. It allows the model to automatically perceive and emphasize the most salient features across different scales for the final diagnosis. Extensive validation on the public Figshare brain tumor dataset, encompassing Meningioma, Glioma, and Pituitary tumors, confirms the efficacy of our proposed framework. Our model achieves state-of-the-art classification accuracy, demonstrating its strong potential for clinical decision support.

We propose a novel contrastive learning framework that utilizes a dynamic label queue to construct multiple positive pairs, enabling more effective and discriminative feature learning for brain tumor image classification.

We enhance the model’s multi-scale perception capability by incorporating a local input stream alongside the global view and introduce an AFF module to adaptively combine these complementary features.
We demonstrate through comprehensive experiments that our unified framework achieves superior performance on the Figshare benchmark dataset, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

Related work

Current research on brain tumor classification from MRI scans can be broadly categorized into traditional machine learning methods and deep learning approaches. Due to the limitations of traditional techniques in feature representation and generalization, deep learning has gradually become the dominant paradigm. It has demonstrated superior performance in handling complex medical imaging data. Recent studies have explored various deep learning architectures to improve classification accuracy. Hasan et al. proposed SSPANet, an explainable attention-based framework. It integrates channel and spatial attention mechanisms to enhance feature representation, achieving 97% accuracy with a ResNet50 backbone while providing improved model interpretability. Tang et al. developed RFENet, a three-branch parallel architecture that extracts complementary features through different network components. By incorporating differentiated data augmentation to address class imbalance, they reached 98.32% macro-average accuracy. Islam et al. leveraged the EfficientNet family for brain tumor classification. Specifically, their EfficientNetB3 model achieved a remarkable 99.69% accuracy through advanced preprocessing on a dataset of 3064 MRI images. Similarly, Celik et al. introduced an ensemble attention mechanism combining intermediate and final-level feature maps. Their method employs co-attention mechanisms to focus on relevant regions, achieving 98.94% accuracy. Balamurugan et al. combined ResNet101 with a channel-wise attention mechanism (CWAM), demonstrating exceptional performance with 99.83% accuracy by emphasizing salient features. While existing brain tumor classification methods have significantly advanced the field, they primarily focus on architectural improvements and attention mechanisms. They often lack explicit strategies to address the fundamental challenges of high intra-class variation and subtle inter-class differences in brain tumor MRI data. The substantial visual differences of the same tumor type across axial, coronal, and sagittal views, coupled with the fine-grained morphological distinctions between different tumor categories, remain significant obstacles. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel contrastive learning framework with adaptive feature fusion. Our approach explicitly learns to minimize intra-class variations while maximizing inter-class distinctions, enabling more robust and discriminative feature learning for brain tumor classification.
Methods
In this section, we provide a detailed description of the AFF-CL framework and its components.
AFF-CL framework

Overview of the AFF-CL Framework. It is composed of a main encoder and a momentum encoder. The main encoder incorporates both global and local inputs. Additionally, the AFF module is designed to achieve adaptive fusion of global and local outputs.

The proposed AFF-CL is an end-to-end contrastive learning framework designed to learn discriminative representations for brain tumor classification. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the framework primarily consists of a main dual-encoder and a momentum encoder. The main encoder takes an input image and its cropped local patch to extract complementary global and local features. Simultaneously, a differently augmented view of the same input is processed by the momentum encoder to produce a consistent feature representation. A core innovation of our framework is the introduction of a label queue, which stores historical features from the momentum encoder along with their labels. This allows the global feature from the main encoder to form not just a single positive pair with its own augmented view, but multiple positive pairs with all features of the same class in the queue, thereby enriching the contrastive learning signal and enhancing feature discrimination. Subsequently, the framework employs two AFF modules. Unlike a simple unidirectional integration, these modules operate collaboratively to enable mutual adaptive fusion between the global and local feature streams. This bidirectional cross-talk allows contextual information from the global view to refine the local features, while simultaneously injecting fine-grained details from the local view into the global representation, achieving a more powerful and multi-scale feature synthesis. Finally, refined fused representation is then directed to the classifier for tumor type prediction.
Contrastive learning framework with a label queue

Brain tumor classification from MRI scans faces two inherent difficulties. The routine acquisition of images from multiple anatomical planes (axial, coronal, sagittal) introduces significant intra-class variation, as the same tumor type can exhibit diverse appearances. Concurrently, inter-class differences are often subtle, primarily defined by variations in tumor location and morphology rather than distinct visual features. To directly address this issue and learn a more structured and discriminative feature embedding, we introduce a contrastive learning framework augmented with a label queue. The core of this framework operates with two encoders: a main encoder and a momentum-updated encoder. The momentum encoder serves as a consistent feature extractor for a dynamically updated queue. As shown in Fig. 1, for an input image, the main encoder produces a global feature representation, termed the query. A strongly augmented version of the same image is processed by the momentum encoder to produce a key feature. Unlike the standard instance discrimination approach that forms a single positive pair (query and its own key), our key innovation lies in leveraging a label queue. This queue maintains a first-in-first-out buffer of the most recent key features encoded by the momentum encoder, with each feature stored alongside its associated class label. This design enables the global query feature to be compared against both its immediate augmented key and a large set of stored keys. Specifically, by referencing the stored labels, the framework can identify and construct multiple positive pairs, where the query is positively contrasted with all keys in the queue that share its label. This mechanism provides a substantially richer supervisory signal. The model is explicitly trained to pull the representations of all samples from the same tumor class closer together in the embedding space, while pushing away samples from all other classes. This directly ameliorates the fundamental challenges of intra-class diversity and inter-class similarity, forcing the model to learn feature representations that are invariant to non-discriminative variations and highly sensitive to the defining characteristics of each tumor class. Formally, for an input image-label pair (x, y), we first derive three different views: a global view  obtained by stochastic data augmentation, a local view  obtained by cropping a region of interest, and a key view  generated through stochastic data augmentation. The global and local views,  and  are fed into the main encoder  to produce their corresponding query feature representations  and . Concurrently, the key view  is processed by a momentum encoder , which is a slowly evolving version of the main encoder whose parameters are updated via a momentum-based main encoder’s parameters, to produce a key feature k. A pivotal component of our framework is the maintenance of a first-in-first-out label queue. This queue dynamically stores the key features k generated by the momentum encoder along with their corresponding ground-truth labels y from recent batches. This design enables our model to construct multiple positive pairs. Specifically, for a given query , it forms positive pairs not only with its own key k but also with all other keys in the queue that share the same label y. The set of these positive keys is denoted as , while the set of all keys with different labels in the queue is denoted as . Therefore, we improve the loss function by introducing a label queue to construct multiple positive pairs, which can be expressed as:where  denotes the temperature parameter. For standard supervised contrastive learning (Supcon), it can be expressed as:Here, Supcon utilizes labels to define multiple positives, its loss computation often involves averaging over all positive pairs in a manner that introduces redundant gradient terms. In contrast, our framework yields a more direct and effective optimization objective for reducing intra-class variance and enlarging inter-class margins, which is particularly advantageous for capturing the fine-grained heterogeneity present in brain tumor MRI slices.
Adaptive feature fusion

The structure of AFF module. The module takes global and local feature tensors ( and ) as inputs and projects them into aligned representations  and . The local features are upsampled to  for spatial alignment. Subsequently, spatial attention weights  and  are dynamically computed and applied via element-wise multiplication () to synthesize the final fused representation .

While the contrastive learning framework effectively narrows intra-class differences and amplifies inter-class separation, it primarily operates on global feature representations. However, discriminative clues for brain tumor classification often reside in fine-grained regional characteristics, including variations in tumor location, morphological shape, and local texture. Global contrastive learning alone may struggle to capture these subtle yet decisive features. To address this limitation, we enhance our framework with multi-scale representation learning through an adaptive feature fusion (AFF) module. Our approach introduces an additional local input, generated by cropping random regions from the original image, which is processed in parallel with the global input by the main encoder. This design yields multi-scale outputs that capture both comprehensive contextual information and detailed local patterns. The core of our solution is the AFF module, which performs adaptive bidirectional integration of these complementary representations. Rather than simply combining features, the module employs a spatial attention mechanism to dynamically recalibrate both feature streams, allowing detailed local characteristics to enhance the global representation and semantically rich global features to enhance local representations. This adaptive fusion creates enriched feature maps that embed crucial fine-grained details within a semantically rich global context. The resulting multi-scale representations not only provide more discriminative features for the final classifier but also create a more structured feature space for contrastive learning. The structure of AFF module is shown in Fig. 2.

Formally, let  and  denote the feature tensors from the global and local branches, respectively. We first project both feature maps into a shared embedding space with aligned channel dimensions. The projected feature  is then resized to match the spatial dimensions of  via bilinear interpolation, producing .The core of the AFF module lies in computing adaptive attention weights that dynamically determine the contribution of each feature stream. The aligned features are concatenated and processed through a gating mechanism:where  represents the spatial attention weights for the global and local features, and  denotes the sigmoid activation function. The final fused representation is obtained through a weighted combination of the original features:where  indicates element-wise multiplication. This formulation enables context-aware recombination of multi-scale features, allowing the model to emphasize the most discriminative characteristics from both global and local representations while maintaining structural coherence for brain tumor classification.

The experiments in this study are conducted on the publicly available Figshare brain tumor dataset. This comprehensive collection contains 3,064 T1-weighted contrast-enhanced axial, coronal, and sagittal MRI slices obtained from 233 patients, with each image having a resolution of 512512 pixels. The dataset encompasses three major brain tumor categories: meningioma (708 images), glioma (1,426 images), and pituitary tumor (930 images). To ensure rigorous evaluation, the dataset is randomly partitioned into training, validation, and test sets with a ratio of 8:1:1. Specifically, 2,451 images (80%) are used for model training, 305 images (10%) for hyperparameter tuning and validation, and the remaining 309 images (10%) are reserved as the test set for final performance assessment. The detailed distribution is summarized in Table 1.
Representative samples of the converted dataset.

Regarding data preprocessing, although the original MRI scans are single-channel grayscale images, we convert them to three-channel RGB format to maintain compatibility with prevalent deep learning architectures that are typically pre-trained on color image datasets. Sample images from each tumor category after preprocessing are visualized in Fig. 3, illustrating the characteristic appearance and structural variations among different tumor types.
Implementation details and evaluation metrics

Implementation Details: The model is optimized using Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with an initial learning rate of 2e-3, momentum of 0.9, and weight decay of 1e-4. Training is conducted for 80 epochs with a batch size of 32. All computational experiments were performed on a system equipped with an Intel i5-12600KF CPU and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5070Ti GPU, running Windows 11. Additionally, the input image size is 224224 pixels. The local view is obtained through center cropping, with a size of 9696 pixels.To mitigate overfitting and enhance model generalization, identical stochastic data augmentation strategies are applied to both the globalviews during training. Specifically, the augmentation pipeline includes a random horizontal flip with a probability of 0.5, a random color jitter (adjusting brightness, contrast, and saturation by 0.4, and hue by 0.1) applied with a probability of 0.8, and a random grayscale conversion with a probability of 0.2. For the queue length, we set it to 8192, with a momentum coefficient of 0.999. The temperature parameter  is 16. During training, the total loss is formulated as the sum of three components: global cross-entropy loss, local cross-entropy loss, and global contrastive learning loss. Each of these three loss functions is assigned an equal weight of 1. Evaluation Metrics: To ensure a comprehensive assessment of model performance, we employ both quantitative and qualitative evaluation approaches. For quantitative analysis, we adopt four standard metrics: Accuracy (Acc), Precision (Pre), Recall (Rec), and F1-score (F1), defined as follows:where TP, FP, TN, and FN, represent true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives, respectively. For the qualitative evaluation, we utilize t-SNE, Grad-CAM, and feature map visualization as evaluation metrics.

To thoroughly evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed AFF-CL framework, we compare its performance with several state-of-the-art methods reported in recent literature on the same Figshare brain tumor dataset. As summarized in Table 2, our method achieves a superior accuracy of 99.35%, outperforming all existing approaches. Notably, the recently proposed C-HGDAT model employs a hypergraph neural network to capture global relationships among image regions, addressing CNN’s limitation in modeling long-range dependencies and achieving 98.22% accuracy. While this demonstrates the value of incorporating global contextual modeling, our framework adopts a different yet more comprehensive strategy. Instead of relying on complex graph construction processes, we directly learn a discriminative feature space through multi-view contrastive learning enhanced by adaptive feature fusion. This allows our model to simultaneously reduce intra-class variance while amplifying inter-class differences more effectively. The performance improvement over other recent methods further validates our architectural choices. Compared to models focusing solely on architectural depth or ensemble techniques, our framework explicitly addresses the fundamental challenges in brain tumor classification: significant intra-class variation and subtle inter-class differences. The integration of contrastive learning with multi-scale feature fusion enables more robust representation learning, particularly for distinguishing morphologically similar tumor types. These results confirm that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art for brain tumor classification.

As demonstrated in Table 3, our AFF-CL framework delivers consistent and substantial performance improvements across distinct backbone architectures, encompassing both traditional CNNs and modern Transformer-based models. Integrating AFF-CL significantly boosts the baseline MobileNetV2 and ResNet50 accuracies from 97.35% and 97.80% to 99.03% and 99.35%, respectively. Furthermore, when applied to Transformer architectures known for capturing long-range spatial dependencies, AFF-CL elevates the performance of ViT-B and Swin-B from 98.06% and 98.19% to 99.35% and a state-of-the-art 99.42%, respectively. Notably, the magnitude of improvement is comparable across lightweight CNNs, heavyweight CNNs, and Transformer models. This uniform gain suggests that the framework addresses a fundamental representation-level bottleneck relative to the poor feature separability in brain tumor MRI, rather than merely compensating for architectural capacity. By explicitly restructuring the feature space through contrastive learning and refining details via adaptive fusion, AFF-CL enables lightweight models to achieve exceptional performance, while pushing advanced Transformers to new heights. This backbone-agnostic improvement confirms that our method effectively solves the core difficulty of feature discrimination, making it a robust solution suitable for various deployment scenarios regardless of the chosen architecture.

The ablation study results in Table 4 reveal the distinct roles of each component in our framework. The CL module acts as the primary performance driver, yielding a significant accuracy boost from 97.35% to 98.77%. This substantial improvement stems from the dynamic label queue mechanism, which fundamentally restructures the feature space by minimizing high intra-class variance, the most critical challenge in brain tumor MRI, thereby establishing a robust global representation. In contrast, the AFF module provides a refined performance uplift to 99.35%, serving as a fine-grained optimization step. While the CL establishes the correct semantic structure, the AFF’s spatial attention mechanism captures subtle, localized morphological details that global features might overlook, ensuring state-of-the-art precision with high stability as evidenced by the low standard deviation. Regarding computational complexity, the introduction of CL increases training Gflops (from 16.53 to 29.34) and training time (from 71.92 to 87.97 minutes) due to the momentum encoder structure; however, importantly, the inference parameter count remains unchanged at 25.83 million and the inference time remains constant at 17.63 ms per image since the momentum branch is discarded during deployment, preserving inference efficiency. The subsequent integration of the AFF module introduces a moderate increase in complexity, raising parameters to 32.22 million, Gflops to 35.09, and total training time to 93.65 minutes due to the additional local processing branch and attention computations. Consequently, the final inference time slightly increases to 19.11 ms per image. This demonstrates that despite the training complexity, our framework remains highly efficient for real-time inference.
Visualization analysis

Feature map visualization of global outputs. (a) Represents the input image. (b) Represents the model with AFF. (c) Represents the model without AFF. The AFF module employs a spatial attention mechanism to dynamically recalibrate two feature streams, enabling refined local features to enhance global representations while semantically rich global features also strengthen local representations. This allows the model to focus more precisely on tumor regions.

Figure 4 presents a comparative visualization of feature activation maps generated by models with and without the AFF module. The visual comparison clearly demonstrates the enhanced focusing capability of our complete framework. Specifically, the input image (Fig. 4 a) displays a coronal MRI scan with a distinct tumor mass located in the upper right quadrant of the brain. As shown in Fig. 4b, the model equipped with the AFF module produces more concentrated and semantically meaningful activation patterns. Observing the multiple feature channels, the high-intensity activation areas consistently and precisely align with the actual physical location of the tumor in the upper right quadrant, while suppressing responses from irrelevant background areas. This refined focus stems from the bidirectional feature integration mechanism, where fine-grained details from the local branch effectively calibrate the global representations. In contrast, the activation maps from the model without AFF (Fig. 4c) exhibit more dispersed and less specific activation patterns. Instead of isolating the pathological region, the activation peaks in the baseline model are scattered randomly across the spatial dimensions, with noticeable and erroneous responses appearing in healthy tissues such as the lower or left hemispheres. This comparison visually confirms that the AFF module enables more effective utilization of discriminative tumor characteristics by adaptively fusing multi-scale features.

Grad-CAM visualization. Our AFF-CL framework demonstrates precise attention on the diagnostically relevant tumor regions in all three views by introducing a contrastive learning framework and the AFF modules.

To further interpret the decision-making process of our proposed framework, we employ Grad-CAM to generate visual explanations of the model’s focus areas. Figure 5 presents the visualization results across three anatomical planes: Axial, Coronal, and Sagittal views. The comparative analysis reveals a consistent pattern across all viewing perspectives. The baseline model exhibits dispersed and sometimes inaccurate attention patterns, with activation areas spreading to non-tumor regions or only partially covering the actual lesions. In contrast, our AFF-CL framework demonstrates remarkably precise and concentrated attention on the diagnostically relevant tumor regions in all three views. This consistent performance across different anatomical planes is particularly significant in clinical practice, where tumors must be accurately identified regardless of the imaging orientation.
t-SNE visualization. Our proposed CL framework significantly enhances brain tumor classification by reducing intra-class variance and increasing inter-class variance.

To intuitively demonstrate the effectiveness of our contrastive learning framework in learning discriminative feature representations, we employ t-SNE to visualize the feature distributions of test samples. Figure 6 presents the comparative visualization between models without contrastive learning (a) and with our proposed contrastive learning framework (b). The visualization reveals significant differences in feature space organization. In the baseline model without contrastive learning, features from different tumor categories exhibit substantial overlap and unclear decision boundaries, particularly between glioma and meningioma classes. This confusion in feature space directly reflects the challenges in distinguishing morphologically similar tumor types. In contrast, our framework with contrastive learning produces well-separated clusters where features of the same tumor type are tightly grouped while maintaining clear boundaries between different categories. This demonstrates that our method successfully achieves dual objectives: enhancing intra-class compactness by reducing feature variations within each tumor category, while simultaneously improving inter-class separation by pushing features of different tumor types apart in the embedding space. The improved feature discriminability can be attributed to our label queue mechanism that enables multiple positive pairs construction, effectively guiding the model to learn invariant representations for the same tumor type while emphasizing discriminative features across different types.

To explicitly validate the superiority of our proposed contrastive learning (CL) formulation (Eq. 2) over the standard supervised contrastive learning (SupCon) loss (Eq. 3), we conducted a quantitative comparison using the ResNet50 backbone. As summarized in Table 6, our proposed CL method achieves an accuracy of 98.77%, outperforming the standard SupCon method, which reaches 97.99%. This performance gap aligns with our theoretical analysis. Standard SupCon involves averaging over all positive pairs, which can introduce redundant gradient terms during optimization. In contrast, our proposed framework leverages the dynamic label queue to provide a more direct and efficient optimization objective. This formulation allows the model to better handle the high intra-class variance and subtle inter-class differences inherent in brain tumor MRI, leading to more discriminative feature representations and higher classification accuracy.

To explicitly demonstrate the unique advantages and novelty of our proposed AFF module, we conducted a quantitative head-to-head comparison against well-established attention mechanisms, specifically the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) and Coordinate Attention (CA). In these experiments, we replaced the AFF module in our complete framework with CBAM and CA, respectively. As detailed in Table 7, our proposed AFF module achieves the highest accuracy of 99.35% and an F1-score of 99.28%, outperforming both CBAM (98.90% accuracy) and CA (99.09% accuracy). Furthermore, the AFF module exhibits a lower standard deviation, indicating higher model stability. The superior performance of our method stems from its fundamental architectural differences. Classic attention modules like CBAM and CA typically operate on a single feature stream to recalibrate spatial or channel-wise weights. In contrast, our AFF module is explicitly designed to handle multi-scale inputs. It performs an adaptive, bidirectional integration of global contextual representations and localized patch features. This unique cross-talk mechanism allows semantically rich global features to enhance local representations, and vice versa. Consequently, the AFF module is significantly more effective at capturing the fine-grained pathological details and morphological distinctions crucial for brain tumor MRI classification than standard single-stream attention modules.

The length of the dynamic label queue determines the number of available positive and negative pairs for contrastive learning, which directly influences feature representation. To justify our selection of 8192 as the default queue length, we conducted an ablation study comparing it with alternative lengths of 4096 and 16384. As demonstrated in Table 8, the model achieves its peak accuracy of 99.35% and highest stability (lowest standard deviation) at a queue length of 8192. When the queue is shortened to 4096, the accuracy drops to 99.03%. This indicates that a smaller queue provides an insufficient number of positive pairs to fully capture the rich intra-class diversity present in brain tumor MRI. Conversely, expanding the queue to 16384 results in a slight performance degradation to 99.16%. This decline can be attributed to the momentum update mechanism: an excessively large queue inevitably retains older, outdated feature representations generated by earlier iterations of the momentum encoder. These outdated features act as noise and interfere with the contrastive optimization. Therefore, a queue length of 8192 strikes the optimal balance between sample diversity and feature consistency.

The cropping size of the local view determines the receptive field for the localized feature stream, significantly influencing the effectiveness of the AFF module. To evaluate this impact and justify our chosen size of 96  96 pixels (derived from the 224  224 global input), we conducted experiments using alternative cropping sizes of 64  64 and 128  128. As shown in Table 9, the 96  96 crop yields the highest accuracy of 99.35% and the most stable performance across all metrics. When the crop size is reduced to 64  64, the accuracy decreases to 99.09%. This suggests that an excessively small local view fails to capture the complete morphological structure and boundary characteristics of larger tumors. On the other hand, expanding the crop size to 128  128 results in a slight accuracy drop to 99.29% and higher variance. This is because a larger local view incorporates more irrelevant background and healthy tissue, making the localized feature representation too semantically similar to the global view. This redundancy weakens the complementary multi-scale interaction that the AFF module is designed to exploit. Therefore, the 96  96 cropping size provides the optimal scale, forcing the network to scrutinize fine-grained pathological patterns without being distracted by excessive global context.
Hyperparameter analysis

Impact of the temperature parameter  on the classification accuracy. The plot demonstrates the sensitivity of the model to different  settings, revealing an optimal operating point at  where the accuracy reaches its peak.

To investigate the influence of the temperature parameter  in the contrastive loss function (Eq. 2), we conducted a sensitivity analysis by varying  from 8 to 24. Figure 7 illustrates these results. The parameter  plays a pivotal role in controlling the smoothness of the probability distribution over the feature space because it determines how much the model penalizes hard negative samples. The experimental results show that the classification accuracy is sensitive to this hyperparameter. Performance initially improves as  increases and reaches a peak of 99.35% at . This suggests that this specific value strikes an optimal balance. It provides sufficient gradients for learning discriminative representations without making the distribution too sharp or too flat. Deviating from this optimum leads to a performance drop. For instance, decreasing  to 8 or increasing it to 20 results in lower accuracy. This underscores the necessity of proper hyperparameter tuning for maximizing the efficacy of the contrastive learning framework.

To further substantiate the robustness and clinical relevance of our proposed framework, we extended our evaluation to a second independent brain tumor MRI dataset. This dataset is notably larger and more diverse than the previous benchmark. It comprises 7,023 images across four distinct categories: No Tumor with 2000 images, Glioma with 1621 images, Meningioma with 1645 images, and Pituitary Tumor with 1757 images. The data was partitioned into training, validation, and testing sets using the same 8:1:1 ratio to ensure experimental consistency. As presented in Table 10, AFF-CL achieves a state-of-the-art accuracy of 99.00% and outperforms competitive methods such as FHD-HybridNet with 98.78% accuracy and TUMbRAIN with 97.94% accuracy. The ability of our framework to maintain exceptional performance on this larger dataset strongly validates its generalization capability. This is particularly evident with the inclusion of a “No Tumor” class. These results confirm that the discriminative features learned via the contrastive label queue and adaptive fusion are robust to variations in imaging protocols and data distributions. This effectively mitigates concerns regarding dataset-specific overfitting.
Limitations

Despite the promising performance of the AFF-CL framework, two potential limitations should be acknowledged. First, the introduction of the dual-encoder architecture and the adaptive feature fusion module inevitably increases the computational overhead. As indicated in the ablation study, the additional branches and attention calculations lead to a rise in model parameters and floating-point operations (FLOPs) compared to a standard single-stream backbone. While this trade-off is justified by the significant gains in diagnostic precision, it requires higher hardware resources during the training phase. Second, regarding the experimental validation, this study followed the prevalent protocol in current literature by employing a random slice-level split on the figshare dataset. Since the dataset originates from 233 patients, a slice-level split carries a risk that different MRI slices from the same patient may appear in both the training and testing sets. Adopting a strict patient-wise split, in which no data from a test patient is seen during training, would likely result in lower classification accuracy compared to the reported 99.35%. This is because the current slice-based approach may allow the model to leverage patient-specific anatomical features, whereas a patient-wise split would demand a higher level of generalization to completely unseen subjects. Future work will focus on validating the framework on strictly patient-isolated datasets to further assess its clinical robustness in more challenging scenarios.
Conclusion

This paper presents a novel contrastive learning framework with adaptive feature fusion (AFF-CL) for accurate brain tumor classification. The proposed method effectively addresses key challenges in medical image analysis by introducing a dynamic label queue that enables construction of multiple positive pairs, explicitly guiding the feature learning process to reduce intra-class variations while enhancing inter-class discrimination. The complementary adaptive feature fusion module further strengthens this capability by intelligently integrating multi-scale features from both global and local perspectives. Comprehensive experiments on the public Figshare dataset demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with 99.35% accuracy using ResNet50 backbone, significantly outperforming existing methods. Ablation studies confirm the individual contributions of each component, while visualization analyses provide compelling evidence of the model’s enhanced focus on discriminative regions and improved feature space organization.
